questions.question: If the coefficients of ${x^{ - 7}}$ and ${x^{ - 8}}$ in the expansion of $\left(2+\frac{1}{3 x}\right)^n$ are equal then n =
questions.question_raw: If the coefficients of {tex}{x^{ - 7}}{/tex} and {tex}{x^{ - 8}}{/tex} in the expansion of {tex}\left(2+\frac{1}{3 x}\right)^n{/tex} are equal then n =
questions.explanation:
We have the general term in the expansion of $ \left( { 2+{ \frac { 1 }{ 3x } } } \right) ^{ n }$ is given by
$T_{ r+1 }=^{ n }{ C_{ r} }(2)^{ (n-r) }\left( \frac { 1 }{ 3x } \right) ^{ r }$
Now x^-r = x^-7 $\Rightarrow$ r = 7
and x^-r = x^-8 $\Rightarrow$ r = 8
$\therefore T_{ 8 }=T_{ 7+1 }=^{ n }{ C_{ 7} }(2)^{ (n-7) }\left( \frac { 1 }{ 3x } \right) ^{ 7 }$
$T_{ 9 }=T_{ 8+1 }=^{ n }{ C_{ 8} }(2)^{ (n-8) }\left( \frac { 1 }{ 3x } \right) ^{ 8 }$
Given, $\frac { ^{ n }{ C_{ 7\quad } }{ 2 }^{ n-7 } }{ { 3 }^{ 7 } } =\frac { ^{ n }{ C_{ 8\quad } }{ 2 }^{ n-8 } }{ { 3 }^{ 8 } }$
$\Rightarrow \frac { n! }{ \left( n-7 \right) !.7! } \frac { { 2 }^{ n-7 } }{ { 3 }^{ 7 } } =\frac { n! }{ \left( n-8 \right) !.8! } \frac { { 2 }^{ n-8 } }{ { 3 }^{ 8 } }$
$ \Rightarrow \frac { { 2 }^{ n-7 } }{ n-7 } =\frac { { 2 }^{ n-8 } }{ { 8\times 3 } }$
$ \Rightarrow \frac { { 2 }^{ n-7 } }{ { 2 }^{ n-8 } } =\frac { \left( n-7 \right) }{ 24 }$
$\Rightarrow 24\times 2=n-7$
$\Rightarrow n=55$

questions.question: The largest term in the expansion of (1 + x)^19 when x = $ \frac 1 2$ is
questions.question_raw: The largest term in the expansion of (1 + x)19 when x = {tex} \frac 1 2{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We have the general term in the expansion of (1 + x)^19 is given by 
T_r+1 = $^{19} C_{r}(x)^{r}$ 
Now $\frac{T_{r+1}}{T_{r}}=\frac{^{19} C_{r}}{_{19} C_{r-1}}(x)^{r}$ 
= $\frac{19 !(x)^{r}}{(19-r) ! \cdot r !} \times \frac{(20-r) !(r-1) !}{19 ! \cdot(x)^{r-1}}$ 
= $\frac{(20-r) x}{r}$ 
= $\frac{(20-r)}{2 r}$ since given x = $\frac{1}{2}$ 
Now $\frac{T_{r+1}}{T_{r}} \geq 1$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{(20-r)}{2 r} \geq 1$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 20 - r $\geq$ 2r
$\Rightarrow$ $r \leq \frac{20}{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $r \leq 6 \frac{2}{3}$ 
Hence the maximum value of r is 6 and hence the greatest term is T_r+1 = T_7

questions.question: If coefficients of three successive terms in the expansion of (x + 1)^n are in the ratio 1 : 3 : 5, then n is equal to
questions.question_raw: If coefficients of three successive terms in the expansion of (x + 1)n are in the ratio 1 : 3 : 5, then n is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let the coefficients of the three successive terms be ^nC_r, ^nC_r+1 and ^nC_r+2 
Now given ^nC_r : ^nC_r+1 : ^nC_r+2 = 1 : 3 : 5
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{^{n} C_{r}}{^{n} C_{r+1}}=\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{^{n} C_{r+1}}{^{n} C_{r+2}}=\frac{3}{5}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{r+1}{n-r}=\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{r+2}{n-r-1}=\frac{3}{5}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ n - r = 3r + 3 and 3n - 3r - 3 = 5r + 10
$\Rightarrow$ n - 4r = 3 ....(i) and 3n - 8r = 13 ....(ii)
Now solving equations (i) and (ii), we get
4r = 4
$\Rightarrow$ r = 1
Now put r = 1 in eqn (i), we get n = 7

questions.question: If 2nd, 3rd and 4th terms in the expansion of (x + a)^n are 240, 720 and 1080 respectively, then the value of n is
questions.question_raw: If 2nd, 3rd and 4th terms in the expansion of (x + a)n are 240, 720 and 1080 respectively, then the value of n is
questions.explanation:
We have the general term of (x + a)^n is T_r+1 = $^{n} C_{r}(x)^{n-r} a^{r}$ 
Now consider (x - 2y)^7 
Here T_r+1 = $^{7} C_{r}(x)^{7-r}(-2 y)^{r}$ 
Comparing the indices of x in x^3 and in T_r+1, we get 
7 - r = 3 $\Rightarrow$ r = 4
Therefore the required term is T_4+1 = T_5 = 18328
We have the general term of (x + a)^n is T_r+1 = $^{n} C_{r}(x)^{n-r} a^{r}$ 
According to the equation
T_2 = $^{n} C_{1}(x)^{n-1} a^{1}$ = 240 $\Rightarrow$ n(x)^n - 1 a = 240 .....(i)
T_3 = $^{n} C_{2}(x)^{n-2} a^{2}$ = 720 $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}(x)^{n-2} a^{2}$ = 720 ....(ii)
T_4 = $^{n} C_{3}(x)^{n-3} a^{3}$ = 1080 $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6}(x)^{n-3} a^{3}$ = 1080 ....(iii)
Multiply equations (i) and (iii), we get $\frac{n^{2}(n-1)(n-2)}{6}(x)^{2 n-4} a^{4}$ = 240 $\times$ 1080 ....(iv)
Now squaring equation (ii), we get $\frac{n^{2}(n-1)^{2}}{4}(x)^{2 n-4} a^{4}$ = 720 $\times$ 720 ....(v)
Now dividing equations (iv) by (v), we get $\frac{(n-2)}{(n-1)} \cdot \frac{4}{6}$ = $\frac{240 \times 1080}{720 \times 720}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{(n-2)}{(n-1)}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6}{4}=\frac{3}{4}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 4n - 8 = 3n - 3
$\Rightarrow$ n = 5

questions.question: If the first three terms in the expansion of (x + a)^n are 729, 7290 and 30375 respectively, then the value of n is
questions.question_raw: If the first three terms in the expansion of (x + a)n are 729, 7290 and 30375 respectively, then the value of n is
questions.explanation:
We have the general term of (x + a)^n is T_r+1 = $^{n} C_{r}(x)^{n-r} a^{r}$ 
According to the question
T_4 = $^{n} C_{0}(x)^{n} a^{0}$ = 729 $\Rightarrow$ (x)^n = 729 ....(i)
T_2 = $^{n} C_{1} (x)^{n-1} a^{1}$ = 240 $\Rightarrow$ n(x)^n-1 a = 7290 ....(ii)
T_3 = $^{n} C_{2}(x)^{n-2} a^{2}$ = 720 $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}(x)^{n-2} a^{2}$ = 30375 ....(iii)
Multiply equations (i) and (iii), we get $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}(x)^{2 n-2} a^{2}$ = 729 $\times$ 30375 ...(iv)
Now squaring equation (ii) we get n^2(x)^2n-2 a^2 = 7290 $\times$ 7290 ....(v)
Now dividing equations (iv) by (v), we get $\frac{n(n-1)}{2 n^{2}}=\frac{729 \times 30375}{7290 \times 7290}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{(n-1)}{2 n}=\frac{5}{12}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 6n - 6 = 5n
$\Rightarrow$ n = 6

questions.question: What is the coefficient of x^2y^2 in (x + 1)^2 . (x + 1)^3?
questions.question_raw: What is the coefficient of x2y2 in (x + 1)2 . (x + 1)3?
questions.explanation:
1
We know that (a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2
(a + b)^3 = a^3 + 3ab^2 + 3a^2b + b^2
Using these formulae, we get
P(x) = (x^2 + 2xy + y^2)(x^3 + 3xy^2 + 3x^2y + y^2)
P(x) = 3xy^4 + 9x^2y^3 + 10x^3y^2 + 5x^4y + x^5 + y^4 + 2xy^3 + x^2y^2
The coefficient of x^2y^2 in (x + 1)^2. (x + 1)^3 is 1.

questions.question: If three successive terms in the expansion of (1 + x)^n a have their coefficients in the ratio 6 : 33 : 110, then n is equal to
questions.question_raw: If three successive terms in the expansion of (1 + x)n a have their coefficients in the ratio 6 : 33 : 110, then n is equal to
questions.explanation:
We have (1 + x)^n = $^{n} C_{0}1^{n}+^{n} C_{1}(1)^{n-1}(x)$ + $^{n} C_{2}(1)^{n-2}(x)^{2}$ + .... + $^{n} C_{r}(1)^{n-r}(x)^{r}$ .... + $^{n} C_{n}(x)^{n}$ 
Suppose the three consecutive terms be T_r-1, T_r and T_r+1, whose coefficients are in the ratio 6 : 33 : 110
Now we have T_r+1 = $^{n} C_{r}(x)^{r}$, T_r = $^{n} C_{r-1}(x)^{r-1}$ and T_r-1 = $^{n} C_{r-2}(x)^{r-2}$ 
Now $\frac{T_{r}}{T_{r-1}}=\frac{^n C_{r-1}}{n C_{r-2}}=\frac{33}{6}$ and $\frac{T_{r+1}}{T_{r}}=\frac{^{n} C_{r}}{^{n} C_{r-1}} \frac{(x)^{r}}{(x)^{r-1}}=\frac{110}{33}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{n !}{(n+1-r) !(r-1) !} \times \frac{(n+2-r) !(r-2) !}{n !}$ = $\frac{11}{2}$ and $\frac{n !}{(n-r) ! \cdot r !} \times \frac{(n+1-r) !(r-1) !}{n !}$ = $\frac{10}{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{(n+2-r)}{(r-1)}=\frac{11}{2}$ and $\frac{(n+1-r)}{r}=\frac{10}{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 2n - 2r + 4 = 11r - 11 and 3n - 3r + 3 = 10r
$\Rightarrow$ 13r - 2n = 15 ....(i) and 13r - 3n = 3 ....(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get n = 12

questions.question: The coefficient of second, third and fourth terms in the binomial expansion of ${(1 + x)^n}$ ( ‘n’, a + ve integer) are in A.P.., if n is equal to
questions.question_raw: The coefficient of second, third and fourth terms in the binomial expansion of {tex}{(1 + x)^n}{/tex} ( ‘n’, a + ve integer) are in A.P.., if n is equal to
questions.explanation:
We have, $ { \left( 1+x \right) }^{ n }=1+^{ n }{ C_{ 1\quad } }\left( x \right) +^{ n }{ C_{ 2\quad } }{ \left( x \right) }^{ 2 }+..........+{ \left( x \right) }^{ n }\quad \quad$
Given that coefficients of $ T_{ 2 },~T_{ 3 }$ and $ T_{ 4 }$ are in A.P
$ \Rightarrow ^{ n }{ C_{ 1\quad } },^{ n }{ C_{ 2\quad } }$ and $\quad ^{ n }{ C_{ 3\quad } }$ are in A.P
$ \Rightarrow ^{ n }{ C_{ 2\quad } }-^{ n }{ C_{ 1\quad } }=^{ n }{ C_{ 3\quad } }-^{ n }{ C_{ 2\quad } }\\ \Rightarrow \frac { n\left( n-1 \right) }{ 2 } -n=\frac { n\left( n-1 \right) \left( n-2 \right) }{ 6 } -\frac { n\left( n-1 \right) }{ 2 } \\ \Rightarrow \frac { n\left( n-1 \right) -2n }{ 2 } =\frac { n\left( n-1 \right) \left( n-2 \right) -3n\left( n-1 \right) }{ 6 } \\ \Rightarrow \frac { n\left( n-3 \right) }{ 2 } =\frac { n\left( n-1 \right) \left( n-5 \right) }{ 6 } \\ \Rightarrow 3n-9={ n }^{ 2 }-6n+5\\ \Rightarrow { n }^{ 2 }-9n+14=0\\ \Rightarrow \left( n-7 \right) \left( n-2 \right) =0\\ \Rightarrow n=2,7$
But n = 2 is not possible as $\quad ^{ 2 }{ C_{ 3\quad } }$is not defined. Hence we get n= 7.

questions.question: The greatest coefficient in the expansion of (1 + x)^12 is
questions.question_raw: The greatest coefficient in the expansion of (1 + x)12 is
questions.explanation:
We have the general term in the expansion of (1 + x)^12 is given by 
T_r+1 = $^{12} C_{r} (x)^{r}$ 
Let the term with greatest coefficient be the term T_r+1 
Now $\begin{array}{ccc} \text {Coefficient} \ \text {of} \ T_{r+1} \\ \hline \text {Coefficient} \ \text {of} \ T_{r} \end{array}$ = $\frac{^{12} C_{r}}{^{12} C_{r-1}}$ 
= $\frac{12 !}{(12-r) ! \cdot r !} \times \frac{(13-r) !(r-1) !}{12 !}$ 
= $\frac{(13-r)}{r}$ 
Now $\begin{array}{ccc} \text {Coefficient} \ \text {of} \ T_{r+1} \\ \hline \text {Coefficient} \ \text {of} \ T_{r} \end{array}$ $\ge$ 1
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{(13-r)}{r} \geq 1$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 13 - r $\ge$ r
$\Rightarrow$ r $\le$ $\frac{13}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ r $\le$ $6\frac{1}{2}$ 
Hence the maximum value of r is 6 and hence the greatest term is the seventh term and T_7 = T_6+1 = $^{12} C_{6}(x)^{6}$ 
Hence greatest coefficient = $^{12} C_{6}$

questions.question: In the expansion of ${(1 + x)^{60}},$ the sum of coefficients of odd powers of x is
questions.question_raw: In the expansion of {tex}{(1 + x)^{60}},{/tex} the sum of coefficients of odd powers of x is
questions.explanation:
We have$\quad \quad { \left( 1+x \right) }^{ n }=^{ n }{ C_{ 0\quad } }+^{ n }{ C_{ 1\quad } }\left( x \right) +^{ n }{ C_{ 2\quad } }{ \left( x \right) }^{ 2 }+..........+{ \left( x \right) }^{ n }\quad .....(i)\quad \\ $
Also$\quad { \left( 1-x \right) }^{ n }=^{ n }{ C_{ 0\quad } }-^{ n }{ C_{ 1\quad } }\left( x \right) +^{ n }{ C_{ 2\quad } }{ \left( x \right) }^{ 2 }-..........+{ \left( -1 \right) }^{ n }{ \left( x \right) }^{ n }\quad ....(ii)\\ $
Now $\left(1-x\right)^n=\left[{}^nC_{0}+^nC_{2}\left(x\right)^2+^nC_{4}\left(x\right)^4+.....\right]-\left[{}^nC_{1\quad}\left(x\right)+^nC_{3}\left(x\right)^3+^nC_{5}\left(x\right)^5+......\right]\\$
Let x=1 and n=60
From equation (i), we get
$ { 2 }^{ 60 }=^{ 60 }{ C_{ 0\quad } }+^{ 60 }{ C_{ 1\quad } }+^{ 60 }{ C_{ 2\quad } }+..........+^{ 60 }{ C_{ 60\quad } }\\ $
$\Rightarrow 2^{60}$=( sum of coefficients of even powers of x ) + ( sum of coefficients of odd powers of x) .....(iii) 
From equation (ii), we get $0=\left[^{60} C_{0}+^{60} C_{2}+^{60} C_{4}+\ldots \ldots .\right]-[^{60} C_{1}+^{60} C_{3}+^{60} C_{5}+\ldots \ldots \ldots .]$
$\Rightarrow 0$ =( sum\quad of coefficients of even powers of x ) + ( sum of coefficients of odd powers of x) .....(iii)
Now subtract equation (iv) from equation (iii), we get
$2^{60}-0=2$ (sum of coefficients of odd powers of x)
$\Rightarrow$(sum of coefficients of odd powers of x $x=2^{59}$)

$\Rightarrow$ n - r + 2 = 2r - 2 and 4n - 4r + 4 = 5r
$\Rightarrow$ 3r - n = 4 ....(i) and 9r - 4n = 4 ....(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get n = 8

questions.question: Find the middle term in the expansion of ${ \left( { \frac { 2a }{ 3 } -{ \frac { 3 }{ { 2a } } } } \right) ^{ 6 } }$
questions.question_raw: Find the middle term in the expansion of {tex}{ \left( { \frac { 2a }{ 3 } -{ \frac { 3 }{ { 2a } } } } \right) ^{ 6 } }{/tex}
questions.explanation:
Since n = 6 is even, its middle term is the 4th term.
We have the general term of (x + a)^n is T_r+1 = $^{n} C_{r} (x)^{n-r} a^{r}$ 
Now consider $\left(\frac{2 a}{3}-\frac{3}{2 a}\right)^{6}$ 
Here Tr+1 = $^{6} C_{r}\left(\frac{2 a}{3}\right)^{6-r}\left(-\frac{3}{2 a}\right)^{r}$ 
Required term is T_4 = T_3+1 = $^{6} C_{3} \quad\left(\frac{2 a}{3}\right)^{6-3}\left(-\frac{3}{2 a}\right)^{3}$ = -20 $\times$ (2a)^0 $\times$ (3)^0 = -20

questions.question: Find the 9^th term in the expansion of ${ \left( { { \frac { x }{ a } }-\frac { 3a }{ x^{ 2 } } } \right) ^{ 12 } }$
questions.question_raw: Find the 9th term in the expansion of {tex}{ \left( { { \frac { x }{ a } }-\frac { 3a }{ x^{ 2 } } } \right) ^{ 12 } }{/tex}
questions.explanation:
We have the general term of (x + a)^n is T_r+1 = $^{n} C_{r}(x)^{n-r} a^{r}$ 
Now consider $\left(\frac{x}{a}-\frac{3 a}{x^{2}}\right)^{12}$ 
Here T_r+1 = $C_{r}\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^{12-r}\left(-\frac{3 a}{x^{2}}\right)^{r}$ 
Hence 9th term = T_6+1 = $^{12} C_{8} \quad\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^{12-8}\left(-\frac{3 a}{x^{2}}\right)^{8}$ = $^{12} C_{8} \times 3^{8} \times(x)^{-12} a^{4}$

questions.question: ${(\sqrt 3 + 1)^{2n}} + {(\sqrt 3 - 1)^{2n}}$is
questions.question_raw: {tex}{(\sqrt 3 + 1)^{2n}} + {(\sqrt 3 - 1)^{2n}}{/tex}is
questions.explanation:
We have $(a+b)^{n}+(a-b)^{n}$
$=\left[{}^nC_0a^n+^nC_1\quad a^{n-1}b+^nC_2\quad a^{n-2}b^2+^nC_3\quad a^{n-3}b^3+\dots\dots\dots\dots+^nC_n\quad b^n\right]+$
$\begin{bmatrix}{}^nC_0&a^n-^nC_1a^{n-1}b+^nC_2&a^{n-2}b^2-^nC_3&a^{n-3}b^3+\dots\dots+(-1)^n\cdot^nC_n\end{bmatrix}^n$
$\\ =2\left[ ^{ n }{ C_{ 0\quad } }{ a }^{ n }+^{ n }{ C_{ 2\quad } }{ a }^{ n-2 }{ b }^{ 2 }+.............. \right] $
Let $a=\sqrt{3}$ and b=1 and n=2n
${ (\sqrt { 3 } +1)^{ 2n } }+{ (\sqrt { 3 } -1)^{ 2n } }=2\left[ ^{ 2n }{ C_{ 0 } }{ \left( \sqrt { 3 } \right) }^{ 2n }+^{ 2n }{ C_{ 2 } }{ \left( \sqrt { 3 } \right) }^{ 2n-2 }{ 1 }^{ 2 }+^{ 2n }{ C_{ 4 } }{ \left( \sqrt { 3 } \right) }^{ 2n-4 }{ 1 }^{ 4 }+......\right]$
$ \\ =2\left[ ^{ 2n }{ C_{ 0\quad } }{ \left( 3 \right) }^{ n }+^{ 2n }{ C_{ 2\quad } }{ \left( 3 \right) }^{ n-1 }+^{ 2n }{ C_{ 4\quad } }{ \left( 3 \right) }^{ n-2 }+.............. \right] $
=2(a positive integer)
Hence we have $(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2 n}+(\sqrt{3}-1)^{2 n}$} is always an even positive integer

questions.question: ${(\sqrt 3 + 1)^{2n}} - {(\sqrt 3 - 1)^{2n}}$ is
questions.question_raw: {tex}{(\sqrt 3 + 1)^{2n}} - {(\sqrt 3 - 1)^{2n}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We have (a + b)^n - (a - b)^n 
= $\left[^{n} C_{0}a^{n}+^{n} C_{1}a^{n-1} b+^{n} C_{2}a^{n-2} b^{2}\right.$ + ..... + $^{n} C_{n}b^{n}$] - [$^{n} C_{0}a^{n}-^{n} C_{1} a^{n-1} b+^{n} C_{2} a^{n-2} b^{2}$ - $^{n} C_{3} a^{n-3} b^{3}$ + .... + $(-1)^{n} \cdot^{n} C_{n} b^{n}$]
= 2[$^{n} C_{1} a^{n-1} b+^{n} C_{3} a^{n-3} b^{3}$ + .....]
Let a = $\sqrt{3}$ and b = 1 and n = 2n
Now we get $(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2 n}+(\sqrt{3}-1)^{2 n}$ = $2\left[^{2 n} C_{1} (\sqrt{3})^{2 n-1} \cdot 1+^{2 n} C_{3} (\sqrt{3})^{2 n-3} 1^{3}\right.$ + $^{2 n} C_{5} (\sqrt{3})^{2 n-5} 1^{5}$ + ....]
Since odd powers of $\sqrt{3}$ will make it an irrational number.
Hence we have $(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2 n}+(\sqrt{3}-1)^{2 n}$ is always an irrational number and it will be positive.

questions.question: If n is a positive integer, then ${(\sqrt 3 + i)^n} + {(\sqrt 3 - i)^n}$ is
questions.question_raw: If n is a positive integer, then {tex}{(\sqrt 3 + i)^n} + {(\sqrt 3 - i)^n}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We have (a + b)^n + (a - b)^n 
= $\left[^{n} C_{0} a^{n}+^{n} C_{1} a^{n-1} b\right.$ + $^{n} C_{2} a^{n-2} b^{2}+^{n} C_{3} a^{n-3} b^{3}$ + ..... + $^{n} C_{n} b^{n}$] + $\left[^{n} C_{0} a^{n}-^{n} C_{1} a^{n-1} b+^{n} C_{2} a^{n-2} b^{2}\right.$ - $^{n} C_{3} a^{n-3} b^{3}$ + .....$\left.(-1)^{n} \cdot^{n} C_{n} \quad b^{n}\right]$
= 2[$^{n} C_{0} a^{n}+^{n} C_{2} a^{n-2} b^{2}$ + ....]
Let a = $\sqrt{3}$ and b = i
Now we get $(\sqrt{3}+i)^{n}+(\sqrt{3}-i)^{n}$ = 2[$^{n} C_{0}(\sqrt{3})^{n}+^{n} C_{2}(\sqrt{3})^{n-2} i^{2}$ + $^{n} C_{4}(\sqrt{3})^{n-4} i^{4}$ + ....]
Since i^2n = (-1)^n = $\pm 1$ 
Hence we have $(\sqrt{3}+i)^{n}+(\sqrt{3}-i)^{n}$ is always a real number and it will be positive or negative depending on the value of n is even or odd.

questions.question: Find the coefficient of x^6y^3 in the expansion of ${ \left( { x+2y } \right) ^{ 9 } }$
questions.question_raw: Find the coefficient of  x6y3 in the expansion of {tex}{ \left( { x+2y } \right) ^{ 9 } }{/tex}
questions.explanation:
We have the general term of $(x+a)^{n} \quad \text { is } \quad T_{r+1}=^{n} C_{r} \quad(x)^{n-r} a^{r}$
Now consider $(x+2 y)^{9}$
Here $T_{r+1}=^{9} C_{r} \quad(x)^{9-r}(2 y)^{r}=^{9} C_{r} \quad(2)^{r}(x)^{9-r}(y)^{r}$
Comparing the indices of x as well as y in $x^{6} y^{3}$ and in $T_{r+1}$ , we get 
$(x)^{9-r}(y)^{r^{2}}=x^{6} y^{3}$
$\Rightarrow r=3$
$\therefore \quad T_{4}=T_{3+1}=^{9} C_{3} \quad(x)^{9-3}(2 y)^{3}$
Hence coefficient of $x^{6} y^{3}=8 .^{9} C_{3}=672$

questions.question: The greatest term in the expansion of ${(3 + 2x)^9}$,when x = 1, is
questions.question_raw: The greatest term in the expansion of {tex}{(3 + 2x)^9}{/tex},when x = 1, is
questions.explanation:
We have the general term in the expansion of $(3+2 x)^{9}$ is given by 
$T_{r+1}=^{9} C_{r} \quad(3)^{9-r}(2 x)^{r}$
Now $\frac{T_{r+1}}{T_{r}}=\frac{^{9} C_{r} \quad(3)^{9-r}(2 x)^{r}}{^{9} C_{r-1} \quad(3)^{10-r}(2 x)^{r-1}}$
$=\frac{9 !(3)^{9-r}(2 x)^{r}}{(9-r) ! \cdot r !} \times \frac{(10-r) !(r-1) !}{9 !(3)^{10-r}(2 x)^{r-1}}$
$=\frac{(10-r) \cdot 2 x}{3 r}$
$=\frac{2(10-r)}{3 r}$ since given x=1
Now $\frac{T_{r+1}}{T_{r}} \geq 1$
$\Rightarrow \frac{(20-2 r)}{3 r} \geq 1$
$\Rightarrow 20-2 r \geq 3 r$
$\Rightarrow r \leq \frac{20}{5}$
$\Rightarrow r \leq 4$
Hence the maximum value of r is 4
Now $T_{5}=T_{4+1}=^{9} C_{4} \quad(3)^{9-4}(2 x)^{4}=126 \times 243 \times 16(x)^{4}=489888$

questions.question: The number 111111…………..1 (91 times)
questions.question_raw: The number 111111…………..1 (91 times)
questions.explanation:
111...111(91times) can be expressed as:-
$\frac{1}{9}\left(10^{91}-1\right)$ $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{9}\left(10^{7}-1\right) \times x$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 1111111 $\times$ x
Where x = (10^7)^12 + (10^7)^11 + ... + 1
